mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz E200 or E250 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M271 engine typically costs £2,500–£5,000 supply only; a reconditioned M274 costs £3,000–£6,000; a reconditioned M264 costs £3,500–£7,500. Used units are available from £1,500–£3,500. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500. Reconditioned M264 engines start from around £2,850–£3,495. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your E-Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz E200 / E250 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation. The M271 (2009–2012) is the least reliable — timing chain stretch is a well-documented issue. The M274 (2013–2019) is more robust but suffers from camshaft adjuster wear, carbon build-up, and oil wicking issues. The M264 (2019–2023) is the most modern and refined, with promising early reliability. With diligent maintenance, all three can be reliable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz E200 and E250 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: M271: timing chain stretch (pre-100,000 miles), plastic oil filter housing cracking. M274: camshaft adjuster wear (cold-start rattle), carbon build-up on intake valves, oil wicking into wiring harness, NOx sensor failure, piston cracking (early examples). M264: complex 48V system, unproven long-term.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz E200 / E250 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: E-Class values remain strong — a well-kept W212 or W213 is often worth £8,000–£25,000+. A £2,500–£7,500 reconditioned engine is 15–40% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example with good bodywork, replacement is often worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz E200 or E250 petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. W212 pre-facelift (2009–2012) uses M271; W212 facelift (2013–2015) uses M274; W213 early (2016–2019) uses M274; W213 late (2019–2023) uses M264. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M271 / M274 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and camshaft adjusters can be replaced — they are wear items. Replacement costs £1,200–£3,000. If the chain fails and jumps timing, the engine is typically destroyed. A preventative chain and adjuster replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£2,500+). At the first sign of rattling or crackling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz E200 / E250 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current E200/E250 petrol market. M271-engined cars sit at the lower end of the range; M264 engines command a premium. M274 engines are typically priced between M271 and M264. Reconditioned M264 engines start from around £2,850–£3,495.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E200/E250 petrol engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Most four-cylinder timing chain jobs are a one-day job.

cta.label: → Compare E200 / E250 petrol engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch and Camshaft Adjuster Wear (M271 & M274)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or crackling noise on cold start lasting 2–3 seconds, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, rough idle, loss of power, engine warning light (cam timing performance codes), Check Engine light
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£3,000 (timing chain, tensioner and camshaft adjuster replacement)
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£5,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the timing chain fails, the engine is typically destroyed

problems.recommendation: The M271's timing chain is a well-documented weak point — "the main problem is the short life of the timing belt, chain, sprockets and tensioner". The M274 also suffers from camshaft adjuster wear, with a "metallic crackling or rumble for 2-3 seconds" on cold start being the classic symptom. If you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain and adjuster replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: M274 — Carbon Build-up on Intake Valves
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, rough idle, higher fuel consumption, hesitation on acceleration
problems.recommendation: Like all direct-injection petrol engines, the M274 suffers from carbon build-up on the intake valves. Regular cleaning and the use of specific additives can help prevent long-term damage. If you notice loss of power or rough idle, have the intake valves inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full carbon build-up guide

problems.title: M274 — Oil Wicking into Wiring Harness (Oil Pump Valve)
problems.symptoms: Various electrical faults, engine warning light, erratic sensor readings, ECU failure
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,000 (harness cleaning or replacement, oil stop adapter installation)
problems.recommendation: The M274 has a known issue where the oil pump valve can leak oil into the wiring harness, potentially reaching the ECU. This "hidden threat" can cause expensive electrical damage. Specialist repair shops install an oil stopper adapter to prevent oil from travelling up the wiring. If you experience electrical gremlins, have the harness inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil wicking guide

problems.title: M271 — Plastic Oil Filter Housing Cracking
problems.symptoms: Oil leaks, visible oil spots under the car, low oil level warning, burning oil smell
problems.recommendation: The M271's oil filter housing was originally made of plastic and is prone to cracking. In some cases, replacing the gasket helps, but the correct solution is replacing the housing. Check for oil leaks around the filter housing area.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: E200 / E250 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Labour-intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
Replace with upgraded units — catch before chain jumps
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option
Walnut blasting — well-understood by Mercedes specialists
Harness cleaning and oil stop adapter — save the ECU
Replace housing — catch before oil starvation

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E200 / E250 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * Late W213 (2019+) with M264 engine — the most modern and refined of the three
* W212 facelift (2013–2015) or early W213 with documented timing chain and camshaft adjuster work — the defining weaknesses addressed
* M274 car with evidence of carbon cleaning and harness inspection
* Full service history with correct MB-spec oil — all three engines are sensitive to oil quality
avoidIf: * M271 with no proof of timing chain work — the chain can fail before 100,000 miles
* Any rattling or crackling on cold start — timing chain or camshaft adjuster wear is likely
* M274 with evidence of electrical issues — oil wicking may have damaged the harness or ECU
* Poor service history — these engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The E200 and E250 petrols are powered by three generations of Mercedes turbocharged four-cylinders. The M271 (2009–2012) is the least reliable — timing chain failure before 100,000 miles is well-documented. The M274 (2013–2019) is a significant improvement but suffers from camshaft adjuster wear, carbon build-up, and oil wicking issues. The M264 (2019–2023) is the most modern and refined, with promising early reliability. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and these engines will reward you with smooth, efficient performance. Skip the history and it will punish you.

cta.label: → Compare E200 / E250 petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz E200 or E250 petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattling or crackling lasting more than 1–2 seconds (timing chain or camshaft adjuster warning — the most critical check on all three engines)
2. Service History — including which generation (M271, M274 or M264) and evidence of timing chain work
3. Oil Leaks (M271) — Any oil under the car? (plastic oil filter housing warning)
4. Electrical Issues (M274) — Any warning lights or erratic behaviour? (oil wicking into harness warning)
5. Performance — Any loss of power or hesitation? (carbon build-up or injector issues)
6. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
7. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (thermostat or cooling system issues)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (NOx sensor, catalyst, O2 sensors)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — three engine families across the E200/E250 petrol's history.
